author: @Sailorman6996
text:
Cool idea that VAWT are more cost effective. If that's true we'll see if they pick up in the future.

A cluster or formation is always better if the formation is on a line and a constant wind direction perpendicular to the formation line. No matter what type - HAWT or VAWT is the same.
If wind direction align with the turbine formation line - the first turbine will interfere with the wind to next turbine and so on. No matter what type - HAWT or VAWT is the same.
Construction of an enormous turbine cluster that change the formation line according to the most efficient at any given wind direction, is my belief adding an enormous cost. And that's why they go with the big space to separate them.

Large scale wind generation has no real future, it has too many negatives for that to happen. it is only political will that has caused the expansion we see today. When the politicians finally see that it is not working and is expensive, support will disappear as fast as it appeared.
It may have niche applications but for grid generation it meets none of the necessary criteria, synchronous (for load and demand balancing), has inertia, can provide reactive power control and support short circuit current levels, criteria that conventional generators have.

author: @kadmow
text:
Rosie Barnes did a video on recycling blades - whether or not to bother, and how landfill in itself can be regarded as "sequestration"  (a least harm disposal)- I have often joked with respect to this.

She was quoting a 20 year design life - Is there any real world study (public) to prove that they are (or are not) meeting their design life - on average?

VAWYs don't have to be low to the ground (lower blades could be surface optimised, while upper blades - clean air optimised), nor do they have to have lower swept area than HAWTs  (design for high centripetal loads - Vestas Rcket is an example of a strange  "blade" -sail - geometry ) - once the diameter of the sweep is above a critical harmonic distance leading edge negative effects will be diminished --   they will tend to have higher stopped drag on the tower head for the same production capacity (a benefit of shorter towers), most likely real - MW capacity commercial units WILL have featherable / variable pitch blades - and absolutely could be self starting, but with buffer batteries, grid connection and inverters, self starting shouldn't be a criteria to "design" less efficient units.

Generator on the ground can be great - or terrible - if the driveshaft adds too much inertia  - or fails in an overpower condition - twisted noodle anyone - hence a really good reason for aerodynamic braking and up-tower emergency brakes.

author: @401ksolar
text:
Started experiments with windmills in the 70's all kinds of designs, however I landed on a very robust design used at the south pole weather station using a 55 gallon steel oil drum cut lengthwise.
The experiment results proved to me that the savonous style tended to survive free run abuse that most other models would fail mechanically.

It ran for 15 years until the used  bearing on the top failed. 
Out ran all others by leaps and bounds.

My thought is the best windmill for you is the one you have working until the next one is built with the lessons from the last.

author: @DavidWilliams-yh6pq
text: Can we alter the shape of vawt or add blades to take weight off thrust berings?

replies.author: @dchall8
replies.text:
Where do you think the weight removed from the thrust bearings would go?  Hmmm.  I suppose you could put a propeller on top of the rotating structure to lift the rotator off the bearing.  
Otherwise it is my understanding that the thrust bearing, and subsequent maintenance on the bearing, is the limiting factor in VAWT development.  The VAWTs tested in Banning Pass back in the 60s ground to a stop when the thrust bearings clogged with sand.  To replace the bearing the entire structure needs to be lifted off the bearing.

author: @deanc6539
text: Complete non sequitur, but I live near the Bay of Fundy with 30 foot tides and I was wondering if you've ever heard of something that uses tidal gravity batteries. The underwater turbines they've trialed all seem to get torn apart pretty quickly, but with a 30 foot lift and drop twice a day, it seems like there could be some potential (no pun intended).

replies.author: @Palisades_Prospecting
replies.text: That is a great idea! I imagine a barge filled with rock until it’s almost sinking. Four steel I-beam legs near each corner attached to electric generators. Wait until the water is at least below the bottom of the barge before you allow it to lower and produce power. And it’s even energy storage if you want to at least for 12hrs. If you secure the legs to the seafloor then you could even power as the bay fills. The problem with those other turbines is they’re biting off more than they can chew. Drop anchor with any ship and I guarantee you the prop will rotate and not get damaged. Scale this up with multiples and you will generate lots.

author: @PoweredbyRobots
text: I remember reading about the leading edge effect in VAWTs, akin to that seen in migrating geese, back around 2005. Place a bunch of them on a gimble table so they can readjust themselves according to the conditions and the trailing turbines experience huge efficiency savings. And the stresses can be shared among the group if they're permitted to change position in the group.

author: @billunderwood5453
text: Years ago I saw a design in Popular Mechanics that I've never seen built. The axle of the generator was mounted at 45 degrees, the gen close to the ground. The rotor had long blades mounted at 90 degrees to each other, from one side to its opposite, such that when one side of the rotor was pointing at the sky, the other side was parallel to the ground, sort of like a badminton shuttlecock held at a 45. The advantage claimed was the it didn't need an enormous pylon sticking up in the air. It could be mounted on a roof ridge and the blades on the high side would reach high for the wind, while the blades on the low side would be parallel to the ridge.

replies.author: @BillMitchell-lm8dg
replies.text:
The blades would undergo cyclical loading on every rotation,
probably leading to fatigue of the blades or bearings - resulting in long-term failure.

replies.author: @ferrumignis
replies.text: ​@BillMitchell-lm8dg I was thinking the same thing as I read the description, there would be an additional bending force on the roots of the blades caused by the rotation trying to force the blades into a flat plane.  Possibly tying the tips of the blades together with a ring structure would help, at the expense of some extra drag.

replies.author: @BillMitchell-lm8dg
replies.text:
.@ferrumignis  - Maybe a toroidal prop would work to tie the tipe of the blades together and help with efficiency, but that would probably add too much weight.

replies.author: @ceptimus
replies.text: You could say the same about coal or oil.  The costs are in getting the stuff out of the ground, processed, and transported to the power stations; also in building, maintaining, and operating the stations - we don't actually pay the ground for the fuel we take from it.

replies.author: @krisztianvirag4807
replies.text: @ceptimus yes, but wind has no costs after a few years and should be cheaper every year

replies.author: @ceptimus
replies.text: @krisztianvirag4807 I suppose the costs are in the maintenance of the turbines, and their associated power distribution cabling.  The cables are distributed over a wider area than with fossil fuel power stations, and they require transformer 'sub stations' to step up the voltages to distribution grid levels.  They're usually installed in remote, often high, countryside, or out to sea, so the costs of getting engineers and equipment to them is much higher.

author: @justtinkering6713
text: I've been building vawt for ten years ,since I retired . Of all the vawt that I have built I like the Sharp vawt the best. It has blades that change pitch passively . It has a low rpm but very high torque. The low rpm lessens the blade fatigue so there are no catastrophic failures. The low rpm/high torque can be translated into high rpm with a transmission. The blades are lift type and I use NACA 0018 blade profile.

author: @hd-be7di
text: An advanced vertical turbine with variable pitch & airfoil shifting blades with wind direction tracking for optimal blade orientation at all phases of the rotation to minimize drag which is what kills efficiency.

replies.author: @eliottc5371
replies.text: Probably too many moving parts

replies.author: @tlangdon12
replies.text: @eliottc5371 It's certainly a more complicated approach. I've been thinking about the problem for a number of years. While pitching the blades uses some of the power that the turbine produces, the result is that turbine can be efficient at a wide range of speeds rather than at one speed. This allows much greater energy to be captured from the wind. I think the number of moving parts could be reduced to a very small number; clearly the blades would have to rotate about their centre of pressure and a precise motor mounted on this centreline of each blade would be needed for motive power. The final moving part would be a rotary transformer to pass power from the ground to the motors.

replies.author: @hd-be7di
replies.text: @eliottc5371  Remove the airfoil shifting blades but keep the variable pitch which does the heavy lifting. The airfoil shifting blade would make it more efficient in a larger range of wind speeds but at the cost of complexity like you said

replies.author: @hd-be7di
replies.text: @tlangdon12  Yep pretty much this ^. The variable pitch will allow the blade to hit the wind at an optimal attack angle for maximum "lift" or pressure and rotate to neutral when the wind direction is opposite and unfavorable for that specific blade on the carousel. I have no idea how many blades would be optimal etc those need to be worked out in engineering programs.

replies.author: @tlangdon12
replies.text: @hd-be7di Particularly with an active pitch VAWT, you want the minimum number of blades because of the cost of actuators. (HAWTs can use a single actuator because all the blades are pitched at once, whereas a VAWT, each blade needs its own pitch angle). But three blades is better than two because it's better balanced. Torque ripple is less of a problem with active pitch control - you can reduce the torque variations using the pitch control rather than increasing the number of blades to get smaller torque impulses. Active pitch control aslo overcomes the problem with starting that VAWTs generally have and also gives better tolerance of high  winds because you can pitch the blades to their minimum drag angle.  Lots of advantages IF you can manage the complexity.
